category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Complex Analysis

questiontext.text: If \(f(z) = u(x,y) + iv(x,y)\) is analytic at a point \(z = x + iy\), then the Cauchy-Riemann equations require:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\) and \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\)

The Cauchy-Riemann (C-R) equations are necessary conditions for a complex function \(f = u + iv\) to be analytic (complex differentiable). They state: \(u_x = v_y\) and \(u_y = -v_x\). If these hold and the partial derivatives are continuous, then \(f\) is analytic. The derivative is \(f'(z) = u_x + iv_x = v_y - iu_y\).

answer.text: \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\) and \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\)
\(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = -\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\) and \(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x}\)
\(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}\) and \(\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y}\)

questiontext.text: If \(f\) is analytic inside and on a simple closed contour \(C\), and \(z_0\) is any point inside \(C\), then Cauchy's Integral Formula states:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(f(z_0) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi i} \displaystyle\oint_C \dfrac{f(z)}{z - z_0}\, dz\)

Cauchy's Integral Formula: for an analytic function \(f\) and a point \(z_0\) inside a simple closed contour \(C\) (traversed counterclockwise): \(f(z_0) = \frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_C \frac{f(z)}{z-z_0}\,dz\). More generally, \(f^{(n)}(z_0) = \frac{n!}{2\pi i}\oint_C \frac{f(z)}{(z-z_0)^{n+1}}\,dz\). This shows that analyticity implies infinite differentiability.

questiontext.text: Liouville's Theorem in complex analysis states that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Every bounded entire function is constant

Liouville's Theorem: if \(f\) is entire (analytic on all of \(\mathbb{C}\)) and bounded (i.e., \(|f(z)| \leq M\) for all \(z \in \mathbb{C}\)), then \(f\) is constant. This has a beautiful application: the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra (every non-constant polynomial has a root in \(\mathbb{C}\)) follows directly from Liouville's theorem.

answer.text: Every analytic function on a bounded domain is constant
Every bounded entire function is constant
Every entire function with a zero is constant

questiontext.text: The residue of \(f(z) = \dfrac{e^z}{z^2}\) at \(z = 0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) 1

\(f(z) = \dfrac{e^z}{z^2} = \dfrac{1}{z^2}\left(1 + z + \dfrac{z^2}{2!} + \cdots\right) = \dfrac{1}{z^2} + \dfrac{1}{z} + \dfrac{1}{2} + \cdots\)

The residue is the coefficient of \(1/z\) in the Laurent expansion, which is \(1\). Alternatively, since \(z=0\) is a pole of order 2: \(\text{Res}_{z=0} f = \lim_{z\to 0} \frac{d}{dz}[z^2 f(z)] = \frac{d}{dz}[e^z]\big|_{z=0} = e^0 = 1\).

questiontext.text: The Maximum Modulus Principle states that if \(f\) is analytic and non-constant on a domain \(D\), then \(|f(z)|\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) Cannot attain its maximum value at any interior point of \(D\)

The Maximum Modulus Principle: if \(f\) is analytic and non-constant in a domain \(D\), then \(|f|\) has no local maximum inside \(D\). The maximum of \(|f|\) on a closed bounded domain is always attained on the boundary. This is a consequence of the open mapping theorem: a non-constant analytic function maps open sets to open sets.

answer.text: Attains its maximum at the center of \(D\)
Attains its maximum at every point of \(D\)
Cannot attain its maximum value at any interior point of \(D\)

questiontext.text: The Laurent series of \(f(z) = \dfrac{1}{z(z-1)}\) valid for \(0 < |z| < 1\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(-\dfrac{1}{z} - 1 - z - z^2 - \cdots\)

Using partial fractions: \(\dfrac{1}{z(z-1)} = \dfrac{-1}{z} + \dfrac{1}{z-1}\). For \(|z| < 1\): \(\dfrac{1}{z-1} = \dfrac{-1}{1-z} = -(1 + z + z^2 + \cdots)\). So \(f(z) = -\dfrac{1}{z} - 1 - z - z^2 - \cdots\) for \(0 < |z| < 1\). The residue at \(z=0\) is \(-1\).

questiontext.text: A Möbius (linear fractional) transformation \(f(z) = \dfrac{az+b}{cz+d}\) (with \(ad - bc \neq 0\)) maps:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Circles and lines to circles and lines

Möbius transformations are conformal maps of the Riemann sphere \(\mathbb{C} \cup \{\infty\}\) onto itself. A key property is that they map circles and lines (viewing a line as a circle through \(\infty\)) to circles and lines. They form a group under composition. Every Möbius transformation is a composition of translations, dilations, rotations, and the inversion \(z \mapsto 1/z\).

answer.text: Circles and lines to circles and lines

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Group Theory

questiontext.text: By Lagrange's theorem, if \(H\) is a subgroup of a finite group \(G\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(|H|\) divides \(|G|\)

Lagrange's Theorem: the order of any subgroup \(H\) of a finite group \(G\) divides the order of \(G\), and \(|G| = |H| \cdot [G:H]\), where \([G:H]\) is the index (number of distinct left cosets of \(H\) in \(G\)). Note: the converse is false in general — not every divisor of \(|G|\) is the order of a subgroup (though Cauchy's theorem guarantees subgroups of prime order).

questiontext.text: A subgroup \(N\) of a group \(G\) is normal if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(gNg^{-1} = N\) for all \(g \in G\)

A subgroup \(N \leq G\) is normal (\(N \trianglelefteq G\)) iff \(gNg^{-1} = N\) for all \(g \in G\), equivalently \(gN = Ng\) for all \(g\) (left and right cosets coincide). Normal subgroups are exactly the kernels of group homomorphisms and allow formation of quotient groups \(G/N\). Every subgroup of an abelian group is normal.

questiontext.text: Sylow's First Theorem states: if \(p\) is a prime and \(p^k\) divides \(|G|\), then \(G\) has a subgroup of order:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(p^k\)

Sylow's First Theorem: if \(p^k\) divides \(|G|\), then \(G\) has a subgroup of order \(p^k\). In particular, if \(p^k\) is the highest power of \(p\) dividing \(|G|\), this subgroup is called a Sylow \(p\)-subgroup. Sylow's theorems (there are three) are powerful tools for classifying finite groups. The number of Sylow \(p\)-subgroups divides \(|G|\) and is congruent to 1 mod \(p\).

questiontext.text: Cayley's Theorem states that every group \(G\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) A subgroup of a symmetric group \(S_n\)

Cayley's Theorem: every group \(G\) is isomorphic to a subgroup of the symmetric group on \(G\) (i.e., \(\text{Sym}(G)\)). If \(|G| = n\), then \(G\) embeds in \(S_n\). The proof uses the regular representation: associate to each \(g \in G\) the permutation \(\lambda_g: G \to G\) defined by left multiplication \(\lambda_g(x) = gx\).

answer.text: A subgroup of a symmetric group \(S_n\)

questiontext.text: In the symmetric group \(S_3\), the order of the permutation \(\sigma = (1\;2\;3)\) (a 3-cycle) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 3

The order of a permutation is the least common multiple (LCM) of the lengths of its disjoint cycles. The 3-cycle \((1\;2\;3)\) has length 3, so its order is 3. Indeed: \((1\;2\;3)^1 = (1\;2\;3)\), \((1\;2\;3)^2 = (1\;3\;2)\), \((1\;2\;3)^3 = e\) (identity). So \(\sigma^3 = e\), confirming order 3.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Ring Theory

questiontext.text: A commutative ring \(R\) with unity is an integral domain if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) It has no zero divisors

An integral domain is a commutative ring with unity \((1 \neq 0)\) that has no zero divisors, i.e., \(ab = 0\) implies \(a = 0\) or \(b = 0\). Examples: \(\mathbb{Z}\), any field, \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\). Non-examples: \(\mathbb{Z}_6\) (since \(2 \cdot 3 = 0\)). Every finite integral domain is a field.

answer.text: Every non-zero element has a multiplicative inverse

name.text: Ring Theory - Prime and Maximal Ideals

questiontext.text: In a commutative ring \(R\) with unity, an ideal \(M\) is maximal if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(R/M\) is a field

An ideal \(M\) of a commutative ring \(R\) with unity is maximal iff \(R/M\) is a field. An ideal \(P\) is prime iff \(R/P\) is an integral domain. Every maximal ideal is prime (since every field is an integral domain), but not every prime ideal is maximal (e.g., \((0)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\) is prime but not maximal).

questiontext.text: Which of the following is NOT a unique factorization domain (UFD)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\)

In \(\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]\), we have \(6 = 2 \times 3 = (1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})\), and these give two essentially different factorizations into irreducibles, so it is not a UFD. In contrast, \(\mathbb{Z}\) is a UFD (Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic), \(\mathbb{Q}[x]\) is a UFD (it's a PID), and \(\mathbb{Z}[x]\) is a UFD (by Gauss's lemma).

questiontext.text: By Eisenstein's criterion, the polynomial \(f(x) = x^4 + 2x^3 + 4x + 2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\)

Eisenstein's criterion with \(p = 2\): \(p = 2\) divides all coefficients except the leading one (\(2 | 2, 2 | 4, 2 | 2\) but \(2 \nmid 1\)), and \(p^2 = 4 \nmid 2\) (constant term). So by Eisenstein's criterion, \(f(x)\) is irreducible over \(\mathbb{Q}\).

answer.text: Reducible as a product of two quadratics over \(\mathbb{Q}\)

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Field Theory and Number Theory

questiontext.text: The number of elements in a finite field \(\mathbb{F}_q\) must satisfy:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(q = p^n\) for some prime \(p\) and positive integer \(n\)

By the classification of finite fields: a finite field has order \(q = p^n\) where \(p\) is the characteristic (a prime) and \(n \geq 1\). For every prime power \(p^n\), there exists a unique (up to isomorphism) finite field \(\mathbb{F}_{p^n}\) (or \(GF(p^n)\)). Fields of prime order \(p\) are isomorphic to \(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z}\).

answer.text: \(q = p^n\) for some prime \(p\) and positive integer \(n\)

questiontext.text: The Chinese Remainder Theorem guarantees a unique solution modulo \(mn\) to the system \(x \equiv a \pmod{m}\), \(x \equiv b \pmod{n}\) when:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\gcd(m, n) = 1\)

The Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT): if \(\gcd(m,n) = 1\) (i.e., \(m\) and \(n\) are coprime), then for any \(a, b\), the system \(x \equiv a \pmod{m}\), \(x \equiv b \pmod{n}\) has a unique solution modulo \(mn\). This generalizes to any number of pairwise coprime moduli. The CRT is fundamental in number theory and has applications in cryptography and computer arithmetic.

questiontext.text: Euler's totient function \(\phi(12)\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) 4

\(12 = 2^2 \cdot 3\). Using the formula \(\phi(n) = n \prod_{p|n} (1 - 1/p)\): \(\phi(12) = 12 \cdot (1 - 1/2)(1 - 1/3) = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 4\). The four integers in \(\{1,\ldots,12\}\) coprime to 12 are: 1, 5, 7, 11. Euler's theorem states \(a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}\) when \(\gcd(a,n)=1\).

questiontext.text: Fermat's Little Theorem states that if \(p\) is prime and \(\gcd(a,p) = 1\), then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}\)

Fermat's Little Theorem: if \(p\) is prime and \(p \nmid a\), then \(a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}\). Equivalently, \(a^p \equiv a \pmod{p}\) for all integers \(a\). This is a special case of Euler's theorem (\(\phi(p) = p-1\) for prime \(p\)). It is widely used in primality testing and modular arithmetic.

questiontext.text: By the Pigeon-hole Principle, if 13 integers are chosen from \(\{1, 2, \ldots, 24\}\), then at least two of them must:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) Have a sum equal to 25

Pair the integers: \(\{1,24\}, \{2,23\}, \{3,22\}, \ldots, \{12,13\}\) — 12 pairs that each sum to 25. If we choose 13 integers from \(\{1,\ldots,24\}\), by the Pigeon-hole Principle, at least two must come from the same pair, so their sum equals 25. The Pigeon-hole Principle: if \(n+1\) objects are placed in \(n\) boxes, at least one box contains \(\geq 2\) objects.

answer.text: Have the same remainder when divided by 13

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Unit 2 - Algebra and Complex Analysis/Topology

questiontext.text: The Heine-Borel theorem states that a subset \(K\) of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is compact if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) \(K\) is closed and bounded

The Heine-Borel Theorem (specific to \(\mathbb{R}^n\)): a subset of \(\mathbb{R}^n\) is compact iff it is closed and bounded. This is specific to finite-dimensional Euclidean spaces; in general metric spaces, compactness is equivalent to completeness and total boundedness (not just closed and bounded). For example, the closed unit ball in an infinite-dimensional Banach space is closed and bounded but not compact.

questiontext.text: A topological space \(X\) is connected if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) The only subsets of \(X\) that are both open and closed are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\)

A space is connected iff it cannot be written as a union of two disjoint non-empty open sets, equivalently, iff the only clopen (simultaneously open and closed) sets are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\) itself. Examples of connected spaces: \(\mathbb{R}\), any interval, \(\mathbb{R}^n\). Example of disconnected space: \(\mathbb{Q}\) (every singleton is clopen in the subspace topology).

answer.text: Every open cover has a finite subcover
The only subsets that are both open and closed are \(\emptyset\) and \(X\)
Every continuous function on \(X\) is bounded

questiontext.text: A topological space \(X\) is Hausdorff (T2) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) For any two distinct points, there exist disjoint open sets separating them

A space is Hausdorff (T2) if for any \(x eq y\), there exist open sets \(U i x\) and \(V i y\) with \(U \cap V = \emptyset\). Hausdorff spaces are important because limits of sequences are unique, compact subsets are closed, and continuous functions into Hausdorff spaces are well-behaved. Metric spaces are always Hausdorff.

answer.text: For any two distinct points, there exist disjoint open sets separating them
Every open cover has a finite subcover
Every sequence has a convergent subsequence
The topology is the discrete topology

questiontext.text: By Tychonoff's theorem, the product of compact topological spaces is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Always compact (even for an infinite product)

Tychonoff's Theorem: an arbitrary (possibly infinite) product of compact topological spaces is compact in the product topology. This is one of the most important theorems in topology and is equivalent to the Axiom of Choice. The product topology is the coarsest topology making all projection maps continuous, and a basis consists of products where only finitely many factors differ from the whole space.

answer.text: Compact only if each factor is metrizable
Always compact (even for an infinite product)

questiontext.text: Which of the following implications about topological spaces is TRUE?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) T2 (Hausdorff) implies T1

The hierarchy of separation axioms: T0 ⊂ T1 ⊂ T2 (Hausdorff) ⊂ T3 (regular) ⊂ T4 (normal). A T1 space is one where every singleton is closed. A T2 (Hausdorff) space is T1 plus the condition that any two points can be separated by disjoint open sets. Every metric space is T4 (normal), so metric ⟹ Hausdorff ⟹ T1. The reverse implications are generally false.
